passages.title: Tikopia
passages.source_html:
Tikopia


The art of traditional tattooing


A


Tikopia is one of the smallest and most remote islands in the South Pacific – a region where neither characteristic is unusual. Just three kilometres long and supporting a 1,200-strong Polynesian* population, there is no harbour or airport, no telephones, shops or hotels. Although originally uninhabited, Tikopia has a long human history, which is still being unravelled. Fragments of pottery unearthed by archaeologists indicate that the first visitors were Polynesian explorers who arrived around 2,000 years ago, but that no permanent settlement was made until the ancestors of the present Tikopians reached the island around the 7th century AD.


B


Because of its tiny size and extreme isolation, Tikopia has had very little contact with the outside world – and for these exact same reasons, anthropologists have long considered it their most desired destination in the South Pacific. Here, and here alone, it is possible to study a traditional Polynesian culture which has changed little for hundreds of years. The most influential of the many scientists who have worked on Tikopia was Professor Raymond Firth, whose pioneering methodology involved living among the Islanders, perhaps for years at a time, adopting their lifestyle and learning their language so as to observe their culture as an ‘insider’. The resulting data were infinitely more detailed than any previously gathered, and were based exclusively on Tikopia, although Firth was the first to admit his lengthy sojourns on the island resulted partly from the difficulties of organising a ship to pick him up from such a remote place.


C


One aspect of Tikopian life which Firth studied was the art of tattooing, a cultural institution in all Polynesian societies. Tattoos were thought to beautify the body and contribute to social cohesion. In the Tikopian language they are called te tau and a similar term is used throughout the thousands of Pacific Islands, whose languages are closely related. When European sailors first arrived in the Pacific, they adopted these skin decorations themselves, though they misheard the local name and called them tattoos. The sailors then carried the art of tattooing across the oceans and today, in some parts of the world, young people consider tattoos the height of fashion.


D


Modern tattoos in places like America and Europe are made with machines and ink but on Tikopia all the equipment used must be made from naturally occurring resource. Essentially, two items are required. ‘Pigment’ is the dye used to stain the skin a black colour, and it is made of the sap from inside the vola tree, a liquid which hardens once it is exposed to the air to form balls. These are subsequently heated on a fire laid in a shallow hole. The fire consists of only smouldering embers because the sap is placed in a coconut shell, which will be destroyed if the fire is too hot. As the sap burns it gives off soot, which settles on a stone above the fire. Once the soot is scraped off and mixed with sea water it is ready for use. The other item is a ‘chisel’, used to push the pigment into the skin. The blade is fabricated from the wing bone of a seabird – the katoko is preferred, as those of the frigate bird are too fragile and tend to break off. This is attached to a long, thin handle, which must be sufficiently heavy to give the tool momentum, so the heartwood of a bush called te ora is chosen to meet this requirement. The two are bound together using fau tree bark torn into strips.


E


Most adult Tikopians wear tattoos and there are strict rules laid down by custom dictating the designs that may appear on various parts of the body. Some men’s tattoos reflect the Islanders’ spiritual links with the vast ocean that dominates their lives, while women’s tattoos are considered more decorative. Men’s facial tattoos are worn by all age groups and consist of small fish shapes. From one to seven may be placed vertically, head downwards, on the forehead, while at the corner of the eye there is usually a single horizontal fish, always facing inwards so it appears to be swimming towards the centre-line of the face. Women rarely wear fish shapes on their faces because fishing is a man’s job on the island. Usually they have bare foreheads and wear the image of a bird at the corner of the eye. But the most common women’s tattoo, just worn by mothers, commences on the chin, below the bottom lip, and extends up the jaw bone as far as the earlobe.


F


The large pattern which covers the chests of men on Tikopia is the most spiritually significant of all the island’s tattoo designs, for within lie the body’s vital organs, the heart and lungs, so the exterior surface is covered with the most time-consuming and precious tattoos. Only older men will be marked with a full chest tattoo.The most common shapes are triangles, which are arranged on a grid of vertical and horizontal lines. Almost as common are the back tattoos worn by middle-aged men. The entire back is covered by vertical lines beginning just below the shoulder blades and terminating at the waistline. The verticals are decorated with short cross lines at intervals of about 100 mm and symbolise te rou mei – sticks of great length – which Tikopian men use to pick the fruit out of the huge breadfruit trees, which, together with fish, form the staples of the Islanders’ diet.


*Polynesian people live on many different islands in the Pacific Ocean.

groups.title: Questions 1-13
groups.source_html:
Questions 1 – 4


Choose TRUE if the statement agrees with the information given in the text, choose FALSE if the statement contradicts the information, or choose NOT GIVEN if there is no information on this

1

Anthropologists like working on Tikopia because it is small and difficult to get to.

正确答案： A. TRUE

定位关键信息：Anthropologists, Tikopia



原文：

B段, “Because of its tiny size and extreme isolation, Tikopia has had very little contact with the outside world – and for these exact same reasons, anthropologists have long considered it their most desired destination in the South Pacific.”


解析：

原文提到由于Tikopia的面积很小，而且与世隔绝，人类学家长期以来一直把它视作南太平洋最理想的研究目的地。


同义词替换：

tiny size and extreme isolation=small and difficult

2

Raymond Firth was the first scientist to work on Tikopia.

正确答案： C. NOT GIVEN

定位关键信息：Raymond Firth


原文：B段, “The most influential of the many scientists who have worked on Tikopia was Professor Raymond Firth, whose pioneering methodology involved living among the islanders, perhaps for years at a time, adopting their lifestyle and learning their language so as to observe their culture as an ‘insider’.” 


解析：原文并未提到雷蒙德·费斯是第一位研究Tikopia的科学家。

3

Raymond Firth’s work included data from other Polynesian islands as well as Tikopia.

正确答案： B. FALSE

定位关键信息：Raymond Firth, other Polynesian islands


原文：

B段, “Here, and here alone, it is possible to study a traditional Polynesian culture which has changed little for hundreds of years. The most influential of the many scientists who have worked on Tikopia was Professor Raymond Firth, whose pioneering methodology involved living among the Islanders, perhaps for years at a time, adopting their lifestyle and learning their language so as to observe their culture as an ‘insider’.The resulting data were infinitely more detailed than any previously gathered, and were based exclusively on Tikopia,”


解析：原文提到Firth收集的数据比任何以前收集的数据都详细得多，并且仅基于蒂科皮亚岛，因此Firth的研究数据仅来自蒂科皮亚岛。

4

The English word ‘tattoo’ has its origins in Polynesian languages.

正确答案： A. TRUE

定位关键信息：tattoo



原文：

C段, “When European sailors first arrived in the Pacific they adopted these skin decorations themselves, though they misheard the local name and called them ‘tattoos’. The sailors then carried the art of tattooing across the oceans and today, in some parts of the world, young people consider tattoos the height of fashion.”


解析：欧洲水手误听了波利尼西亚语对纹身的称呼，将其称为“tattoo”。

Questions 5 – 9


Complete the diagram. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS in each gap

5

is collected from here

hard of sap

bowl made from a

blade made from katoko's

made from the of a small tree

正确答案： 5 soot  |  6 balls  |  7 coconut shell  |  8 wing bone  |  9 heartwood
Question 5

定位关键信息：Pigment production


原文：D段, “As the sap burns it gives off soot, which settles on a stone above the fire. ” 


解题思路：原文提到soot在火上方的石头上。

Question 6

定位关键信息：sap


原文：D段, “‘Pigment’ is the dye used to stain the skin a black colour, and it is made of the sap from inside the vola tree, a liquid which hardens once it is exposed to the air to form balls.” 


解析： 原文提到sap是由液体暴露在空气后硬化形成的球制作而成。

Question 7

定位关键信息：bowl


原文：D段, “The fire consists of only smouldering embers because the sap is placed in a coconut shell, which will be destroyed if the fire is too hot.  ” 


解析： 原文提到sap放在coconut shell制成的碗中。

Question 8

定位关键信息：blade, katoko


原文：D段, “The blade is fabricated from the wing bone of a seabird – the katoko is preferred, as those of the frigate bird are too fragile and tend to break off.” 


解析：原文提到blade是由海鸟的wing bone制作而成。

Question 9

定位关键信息：small tree

 

原文：D段, “This is attached to a long, thin handle, which must be sufficiently heavy to give the tool momentum, so the heart wood of a bush called te ora is chosen to meet this requirement.”

 

解析：原文提到工具的把手是由灌木的heart wood制作而成。

Questions 10 – 13


Complete the table. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS in each gap.

10
Location of TattooDesign of TattooAges/types of adults who wear itMeaning of Tattoo
Men's Facefish on and corner of eyesall agesshows their job
Women's Faceline from chin to earonly decoration
Men's Chest placed on gridolder mencelebrates most important organs
Men's Back running from shoulder blades to waistmiddle-aged and olderrepresent long sticks used to get food
正确答案： 10 forehead  |  11 mothers  |  12 triangles  |  13 vertical lines
Question 10

定位关键信息：men’s face, fish


原文：E段, “Men’s facial tattoos are worn by all age groups and consist of small fish shapes. From one to seven may be placed vertically, head downwards, on the forehead, while at the corner of the eye there is usually a single horizontal fish, always facing inwards so it appears to be swimming towards the centre-line of the face. ” 


解析：原文提到男性脸部的纹身通常是额头和眼角处的一条鱼。

Question 11

定位关键信息：women’s face, chin, ear


原文：E段, “Women rarely wear fish shapes on their faces because fishing is a man’s job on the island. Usually they have bare foreheads and wear the image of a bird at the corner of the eye. But the most common women’s tattoo, just worn by mothers, commences on the chin, below the bottom lip, and extends up the jaw bone as far as the earlobe.” 


解析： 原文提到母亲通常会在脸颊到耳朵纹上线条的纹身。

Question 12

定位关键信息：men's chest


原文：F段, “Only older men will be marked with a full chest tattoo. The most common shapes are triangles, which are arranged on a grid of vertical and horizontal lines.” 


解析： 原文提到男性会在胸口纹三角形的纹身。

Question 13

定位关键信息：men's back, shoulder, waist


原文：F段, “Almost as common are the back tattoos worn by middle-aged men. The entire back is covered by vertical lines beginning just below the shoulder blades and terminating at the waistline.” 


解析： 原文提到中年男子会在后背从肩膀到腰部的位置纹垂直的竖线。
